summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PARENTING – Interim decision – Where UK Court orders permitted the mother relocate with the child to Australia until 19 February 2026 – Where the mother seeks to apply for a permanent residency visa for Australia – Where the father objects – Consideration of jurisdiction – Where the child is found to be habitually resident in Australia – Orders made granting sole parental responsibility to the mother for the limited purpose of immigration and application for a visa – Orders made permitting the mother to have the child remain in Australia after 19 February 2026 on an interim basis

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

BOYLE J:

These are my ex tempore reasons taken from the transcript, corrected for grammatical errors and to make my oral reasons more readable.
The mother has applied to this Court for orders for parental responsibility for X, born in 2018, to permit her to apply for a permanent residency visa for Australia. The father objects to that occurring.
The mother has filed an Application and an affidavit in support of the orders that she seeks. She also relies on a Case Outline document.
Directions were made for the father to file a Response and an affidavit. He has not done so. He says that was due to the difficulties in time, both in the sense of the shortness of time and that he is in the United Kingdom (“UK”).
The father has provided what I will refer to as “written submissions” in the matter. He annexed to some email correspondence between the parties, which appeared to me to be negotiation between the parties about arrangements for X. When I raised that with the parties, both agreed that was the case. I have therefore had no regard to that material so that the parties can keep negotiation to resolve the matter confidential.
The other document provided was what appears to be notes of a judgment of the Family Court in City B. The document is not a sealed copy of a judgment, nor is it under any cover recognisably from the Court. It is not clear to me, on the face of the document, what the origin is. Neither parent has a copy of the judgment of the Family Court in City B. I accept the father was doing his best by providing that material. I have had no regard to it because I am not able to be satisfied about the origin of the document, the father himself being unaware of how it came about.
There were proceedings in the UK, which concluded with a decision of the Family Court at City B in June 2024. Both parties were represented in those proceedings. The orders made grant the mother permission to relocate with X to Australia for a period expiring 19 February 2026. The mother is directed to return with X to the UK prior to the expiry of her visa unless she has the consent of the father to stay longer or has made an application to the court for the issue to be considered further.
The orders make provision for communication between X and her father by three video calls per week, one week to 14 days of the year in the UK, two occasions of two to four weeks each year with the father in Australia, and as otherwise agreed. These are framed in the orders as “minimum requirements”.
There are lengthy recitals to the orders. There is a reference within the recitals to the orders of a written judgment dated June 2024, which found that:
... [X’s] best interests are served by her relocating to Australia with her mother for the period as outlined below.
This is the judgment that neither party has a copy of. Within the recitals to the orders is this:
Upon this matter having proceeded as a contested final hearing where the court considered the mother’s application for a specific issue order permitting her to relocate with [X] to Australia for a period of 2 years, and the mother will consider further at that stage whether she wishes to remain permanently with [X] in Australia or move back to [the UK].
The orders are framed as “final orders”. The orders make reference to the possibility of future applications. Order 7 states:
In respect of any further applications relating to [X], in the event that it is found that [the UK] retains jurisdiction, the matter shall be reserved to [the District Judge], if at all possible, and save for emergency applications.
The mother submitted that prior to making this application in Australia, she had written to the Court in City B seeking a relisting before the District Judge as indicated at Order 7. She was advised the proceedings had concluded and she would need to make a further application. She then applied to this Court.
This issue demonstrates the difficulties in this matter, where both parties are self-represented. What they are dealing with are complex issues of international jurisdiction, and comity between courts. It is hoped that in any further proceedings, the parties may obtain legal representation.

BACKGROUND

The parties were married in 2015 and separated when X was two years old. X has lived primarily with her mother. She has spent regular time with her father. The mother came to Australia on a temporary skills visa with X. The mother is a medical professional . She has been employed throughout the time she has been present in Australia.
X lives with her mother and her mother’s partner. The mother attests to the child having a familiar and comfortable relationship with her partner. He provides practical day-to-day support within the home. The mother meets the child’s needs globally.
X has been attending C School. The mother annexed to her affidavit a report on her second semester of 2025. The report is very positive about X. It describes X as an engaged and interested student. The report notes one day’s absence over the whole of the second semester. The report supports the mother’s evidence and submission that X has established routines, friendships and a sense of stability at school, and in her home in NSW.
The mother has complied with the orders for time between X and her father. The father suggested in submissions that she had not. The mother has not yet arranged for X’s time with her father in 2026. She was in the UK from December 2024 until mid-January 2025 and is thus in compliance with Order 5(b) of the orders of the Family Court at City B.
The orders further provide for time between X and the father to occur on two occasions for two to four weeks annually in Australia. The father has not complied with this order for time with his daughter.
The mother, as is clear from the recitals and the body of the orders, was required to notify the Child Maintenance Service in the UK that she was relocating to Australia and did not wish to obtain child support, or child maintenance as referred to, from the father so he could use those funds to travel to Australia to see his daughter. It was conceded by the father that the mother has complied with all requirements, where he has not.
The mother has annexed to her affidavit a transcription of WhatsApp messages between herself and the father. The father was asked to confirm if the transcription is correct because it is not done by way of a screenshot, but transcribed. The father confirmed that the contents were correct. The mother, through the messages, has sought the father’s consent to apply for permanent residency for X. As she noted in submissions, she has no guarantee the application for permanent residency will be successful.
The WhatsApp communication from 3 and 4 December 2025 sets out her providing to the father the form to apply for the visa for X, and advising him of the steps required. The mother highlighted the difficulties for X if her visa expires, and she sought an urgent response from the father:
... otherwise, we will end up in the same situation like last time!
I take this as a reference to the previous court proceedings in the United Kingdom. The mother notes in her message to the father:
Still that doesn’t mean that if we decided to come back to UK the permanent residency status will stop us or [X]. As you know she had [UK citizenship] and that didn’t stop her leaving UK. Thus same way permanent residency only means we can get free health care and education here.
The father responded the following day:
We discussed this before and agreed on a 5 year plan and hence we got to this point.... until she finishes her primary education.

... the need for me to sign for her permanent visa is a change to what was agreed and brought us to court in the 1st instance.

...

I am therefore happy to sign the form (temporary visa) as discussed and agreed before.

I accept that the father was in agreement at the time of the messages in December 2025 with X remaining in Australia until the end of her primary school. She is currently aged eight. He does not agree to applications for permanent residency as opposed to temporary residency. In submissions before me, the father had no difficulty with X remaining in Australia until at least 17 April 2026 when, as I understand it, there are school holidays in the UK.
The issue to be determined is whether the Court has jurisdiction to make orders with respect to parental decision-making for X on an urgent basis. The mother seeks parental responsibility to enable her to apply for permanent residency and complete all necessary documentation in support of that application. I accept the urgency arises because the mother has a limited opportunity to apply for a skilled visa. It appears she has provided an expression of interest and received the invitation to apply as a consequence of that.

THE LAW

At the time of the mother filing the application in this Court, X was present in Australia. Section 69E of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) provides:
(1) Proceedings may be instituted under this Act in relation to a child only if:
(a) the child is present in Australia on the relevant day
‘Relevant day’ means the day on which the application is filed.
The legislation notes that section 69E is to be read subject to Division 4, Part VIIIAA, which was inserted into the Act in 2003 by the Family Law Amendment (Child Protection Convention) Act 2002 (Cth). This division was intended to give effect to Australia’s treaty obligations under The Convention of 19 October 1996 on Jurisdiction, Applicable Law, Recognition, Enforcement and Cooperation in respect of Parental Responsibility and Measures for the Protection of Children. That Convention is generally referred to as the Child Protection Convention 1996, which is the approach I shall take in these reasons.
The definitions to Division 4, Part XIIIAA include:
Commonwealth personal protection measure relating to a child means a measure (within the meaning of the Child Protection Convention) under this Act that is directed to the protection of the person of the child.
Relevantly, section 111CD sets out:
(1) A court may exercise jurisdiction for a Commonwealth personal protection measure only in relation to:

(a) a child who is present and habitually resident in Australia; or

(b) a child who is present in Australia and habitually resident in a Convention country, if:

(i) the child’s protection requires taking the measure as a matter of urgency; or

(ii) the measure is provisional and limited in its territorial effect to Australia; or

(iii) the child is a refugee child; or

(iv) a request to assume jurisdiction is made to the court by, or at the invitation of, a competent authority of the country of the child’s habitual residence; or

(v) a competent authority of the country of the child’s habitual residence agrees to the court assuming jurisdiction; or

(vi) the court is exercising jurisdiction in proceedings concerning the divorce or separation of the child’s parents or the annulment of their marriage (but see subsection (3)); or

(c) a child who is present in a Convention country, if:

(i) the child is habitually resident in Australia; or

(ii) the child has been wrongfully removed from or retained outside Australia and the court keeps jurisdiction under Article 7 of the Child Protection Convention; or

(iii) a request to assume jurisdiction is made to the court by, or at the invitation of, a competent authority of the country of the child’s habitual residence or country of refuge; or

(iv) a competent authority of the country of the child’s habitual residence or country of refuge agrees to the court assuming jurisdiction; or

(v) the child is habitually resident in a Convention country and the court is exercising jurisdiction in proceedings concerning the divorce or separation of the child’s parents or the annulment of their marriage (but see subsection (3));

...
There are various other subsections thereafter which do not appear to have any application to X, referring to refugee children and the like:
(2) A court may only exercise jurisdiction in accordance with subparagraph (1)(b)(ii) if the measure is not incompatible with a foreign measure already taken by a competent authority of a Convention country under Articles 5 to 10 of the Child Protection Convention.

(3) A court may only exercise jurisdiction in accordance with subparagraph (1)(b)(vi) or (c)(v) for a Commonwealth personal protection measure relating to a child if:

(a) one or both of the child’s parents are habitually resident in Australia when the proceedings referred to in that subparagraph begin; and

(b) one or both of the parents have parental responsibility for the child; and

(c) the jurisdiction of the court to take the measure is accepted by the parents and each other person with parental responsibility for the child; and

(d) the exercise of jurisdiction to take the measure is in the best interests of the child; and

(e) the proceedings on the application for divorce or separation of the child’s parents or the annulment of their marriage have not been finalised.

(4) Paragraphs 111CD(1)(a) to (d) are subject to the limitations in sections 111CE, 111CF and 111CH.
Looking at the terms of the section, a matter for consideration is the meaning of a Commonwealth personal protection order under the Child Protection Convention. Turning to the Convention itself is necessary to understand the various terms referred to within the definition to Division 4, Part XIIIAA, Article 1(1) of the 1996 Convention recites the objects of the Convention to include:
a) To determine the State whose authorities have jurisdiction to take measures directed to the protection of the person or the property of the child;
...

b) To determine the law applicable to parental responsibility;

Article 1(2) provides that:
For the purposes of this Convention, the term parental responsibility’ includes parental authority, or any analogous relationship of authority determining the rights, powers and responsibilities of parents, guardians or other legal representatives in relation to the person or the property of the child.
Article 3 provides that:
The measures referred to in Article 1 may deal in particular with –

a) the attribution, exercise, termination or restriction of parental responsibility, as well as its delegation;

b) rights of custody, including rights relating to the care of the person of the child and, in particular, the right to determine the child's place of residence, as well as rights of access including the right to take a child for a limited period of time to a place other than the child's habitual residence;

Considering the issues in this matter, the mother seeks an order for parental responsibility or parental decision-making with respect to X. Parenting orders are orders made by this Court under section 64B of the Act, including orders dealing with the person with whom a child is to live, spend time, allocation of parental decision-making, and the form of consultation required between parents. Section 65D refers to the Court’s powers to make a parenting order, including such parenting order as it thinks proper.
Considering these matters, I accept that a Commonwealth personal protection measure, as referred to in section 111CD(1) of the Act for the implementation of the 1996 Convention, includes a parenting order as defined by section 64B. I note the decision of Bunyon & Lewis (No 3) [2013] FamCA 888 of Bennett J, where she deals with this aspect of the meaning of the Commonwealth protection measure and the interplay of division 4, Part VIIIAA, with the Child Protection Convention.
Subdivision B refers to jurisdiction for the person of a child, and section 111CC provides:
This Subdivision applies only if an issue under this Act is whether a court, as opposed to any of the following authorities, has jurisdiction to take measures directed to the protection of the person of a child:

(a) a central authority or competent authority of a Convention country;

(b) a competent authority of a non‑Convention country.
Considering then the application of section 111CD to the facts of this matter, the first basis upon which a court may exercise jurisdiction for a Commonwealth personal protection measure is in relation to a child who is present and habitually resident in Australia. This requires a consideration of whether X is habitually resident in Australia. A child’s habitual residence is a question of fact. The phrase is to be understood according to the ordinary and natural meaning of the words: Cooper & Casey [1995] FamCA 2; (1995) FLC 92-575. That case at 5-6 sets out the following:
2. Habitual residence is a term referring, when it is applied in the context of married parents living together, to their abode in a particular place or country which they have adopted voluntarily and for settled purposes as part of the regular order of their life for the time being whether it is of short or long duration.

All that the law requires for a “settled purpose” is that the parents’ shared intentions in living where they do should have a sufficient degree of continuity about them to be properly described as settled.

The High Court considered the issue of habitual residence in 2009 in LK v Director-General, Department of Community Services [2009] HCA 9; (2009) 237 CLR 582. It is clear from that decision that:
44. ... an inquiry should take into account all relevant factors, including settled purpose, the actual and intended length of stay in a state, the purpose of the stay, the strength of ties to the state and to any other state ... the degree of assimilation into the state, including living and schooling arrangements, and cultural, social and economic integration. ...
Habitual residence does not require a minimum amount of time in a particular place. Rather, it requires a consideration of the factors referred to, to come to an understanding of whether, as an issue of fact, the child is now habitually resident.
Considering that issue, the mother’s evidence refers to X’s circumstances. She has been living in Australia for the past 18 months. The evidence discloses that she is attending school regularly; she is doing well at school and is a dedicated student. She has close relationships with her mother and a good relationship with her mother’s partner. X spends time and communicates with her father pursuant to the orders, albeit it is not as frequent as it should be because the father has not exercised the time he could have done. This engagement of X within her community supports that she is settled here. I accept she has acquired habitual residence in Australia.
The situation came about through the mother obtaining orders from the Family Court in City B which permitted her to relocate X to Australia. The orders made contemplate that occurring until 19 February 2026, or longer if the parties agree, or the mother makes an application. Properly, she is now making that application to a court. She has done so within the timeframe set out by the City B Court orders, that is, prior to any requirement for her to return to Australia.
The mother has properly sought the father’s agreement to extend the time for X to remain in Australia. That appears from his submissions to now to be at least until April 2026, although I note the father’s reference in December 2025 to a five-year plan to the conclusion of X at primary school.
I do not yet have evidence before me from the father as he did not file an affidavit, nor a Response. However, I understand from his position at this Court that the timeframe contemplated by him is to April 2026 and not, at the present time, beyond that.
For these reasons, I propose making the orders that are sought by the mother, which I am satisfied are required for X. They are within the definition of a “Commonwealth personal protection measure” in relation to the child who is present in Australia and habitually resident in Australia. I propose reframing somewhat the orders sought by the mother, which will provide for her to be able to do all things necessary to apply for permanent residency for X in Australia.
As the mother has quite rightly said in submissions, this is not determinative of where X lives long-term. Rather, it gives the option for the mother to remain with X in Australia for a longer period. It is protective of X in that it gives her access to Medicare, if she were to suffer any major health issue, appreciating that the mother has also taken out private health insurance for X, quite properly, to ensure her proper care whilst she is in Australia.
I propose making directions for the mother to file and serve an Amended Application setting out what orders she seeks on a longer-term basis. The father can then be in a position to respond, having given proper consideration to his position about X’s future.
I will order that the mother be permitted to have X remain in Australia after 19 February 2026 on an interim basis. It is not a permanent order. I do so on the basis that the mother is properly making an application for a court to determine what should ultimately happen for X.
